questions.q: Two positive point charges are brought closer together. What happens to the electrostatic force between them?
options: It decreases because like charges weaken each other
It stays the same because charge is conserved
It increases, since Coulomb force grows as the separation decreases
It becomes attractive at very short distances
questions.explanation: Coulomb's law states F = kq₁q₂/r², so halving the distance quadruples the repulsive force. The force between two like charges never becomes attractive in classical electrostatics, no matter how small the separation; that distractor confuses electrostatics with nuclear forces between protons.

questions.q: Which statement correctly describes electric field lines around an isolated positive point charge?
options: They form closed loops around the charge
They point radially outward, away from the charge
They point radially inward, toward the charge
They are circles centered on the charge
questions.explanation: By convention, electric field lines show the direction of the force on a positive test charge, so they radiate outward from a positive charge and terminate on negative charges. Closed loops describe magnetic field lines (or induced electric fields), not the electrostatic field of a point charge, which begins on the charge and extends to infinity.

questions.q: Three identical resistors are connected in parallel across a battery. Compared to a single resistor alone, the total resistance of the parallel combination is:
options: Three times the single resistance
Equal to the single resistance
Unchanged, but the current is one third
One third of the single resistance
questions.explanation: For identical resistors in parallel, R_total = R/n, so three resistors give R/3 because the current has three equal paths to flow through. Tripling the resistance is what happens in series, where the same current must pass through each resistor in turn — a common point of confusion between the two configurations.

questions.q: Which statement expresses Gauss's law for magnetism in qualitative terms?
options: There are no magnetic monopoles; magnetic field lines always form closed loops
The magnetic flux through a closed surface equals the enclosed current
A changing magnetic field creates an electric field
Magnetic field strength decreases with the square of distance from any magnet
questions.explanation: Gauss's law for magnetism (∇·B = 0) says the net magnetic flux through any closed surface is zero, meaning isolated north or south poles do not exist and field lines must close on themselves. The statement about changing magnetic fields creating electric fields is Faraday's law, a different one of Maxwell's equations.

questions.q: A bar magnet is pushed north-pole-first into a coil of wire connected to a galvanometer. What does Lenz's law predict?
options: The induced current flows so as to attract the magnet into the coil faster
No current is induced unless the coil is also moving
The induced current creates a magnetic field that opposes the magnet's approach
The induced current direction depends only on the coil's resistance
questions.explanation: Lenz's law states that an induced current always opposes the change in magnetic flux that produces it, so the coil's near face becomes a north pole that repels the incoming magnet. If the induced current aided the magnet's motion instead, the magnet would accelerate and generate ever more energy from nothing, violating conservation of energy. Only relative motion between magnet and coil matters, so the coil itself need not move.

questions.q: A charged particle moves at constant velocity parallel to a uniform magnetic field. What is the magnetic force on it?
options: Maximum, because the particle is moving
Zero, because the force depends on the component of velocity perpendicular to the field
Directed along the field lines
Directed opposite to the particle's motion
questions.explanation: The magnetic force is F = qv × B, whose magnitude is qvB·sinθ; when velocity is parallel to the field, θ = 0 and the force vanishes. Simply moving is not enough — only the velocity component perpendicular to B produces a force, which is why the force is maximal at 90°, not for parallel motion.

questions.q: What did Maxwell's addition of the displacement current term to Ampère's law make possible to predict?
options: The quantization of electric charge
The existence of magnetic monopoles
Superconductivity at low temperatures
Self-propagating electromagnetic waves traveling at the speed of light
questions.explanation: The displacement current term means a changing electric field generates a magnetic field, which together with Faraday's law allows electric and magnetic fields to regenerate each other and propagate as waves at speed c. This unified light with electromagnetism. Maxwell's equations actually assume there are no magnetic monopoles, so predicting them is the opposite of what the equations state.

questions.q: A 60 W light bulb operates on a 120 V supply. What current does it draw?
options: 2 A
questions.explanation: Electric power is P = VI, so I = P/V = 60/120 = 0.5 A. The answer 2 A comes from inverting the ratio and computing V/P instead of P/V; checking units shows watts divided by volts gives amperes.

questions.q: Why is electrical power transmitted over long distances at very high voltage?
options: For a given power, higher voltage means lower current, which reduces resistive losses (I²R) in the lines
Higher voltage makes electrons travel much faster through the wire
High voltage eliminates the resistance of the transmission wires
Transformers can only operate at high voltages
questions.explanation: Transmitted power is P = VI, so raising the voltage allows the same power to be delivered with much smaller current, and the heat dissipated in the line, P_loss = I²R, drops with the square of the current. High voltage does not remove the wire's resistance — the resistance is fixed by the wire's material and geometry; it only reduces the current flowing through that resistance.

questions.q: A hollow conducting shell carries no net charge, and a point charge +q is placed at its center. What is the induced charge on the inner surface of the shell?
options: +q
Zero, because the shell is neutral overall
−q
−q/2, with the other half on the outer surface
questions.explanation: The electric field inside the conducting material must be zero, so a Gaussian surface drawn within the conductor must enclose zero net charge; this forces exactly −q to accumulate on the inner surface, leaving +q on the outer surface. Overall neutrality of the shell does not mean each surface is uncharged — it only means the inner and outer surface charges sum to zero.
